Q1 — Place: There was a wooden floor in the basement of my old school where the gym class met. Smooth, slightly springy, with a particular give I've never felt anywhere else. I used to find excuses to be there alone — to drop something I'd 'forgotten,' to volunteer to put chairs away. I'd run my socked foot across it and just feel the slide. I remember the feeling of being barefoot on it once and how my whole body settled into the floor differently. The room smelled faintly of rubber and chalk. My body remembered that floor for years.
Q2 — Habit: I cannot walk in a straight line when music plays in a store. My friends point this out constantly. I'll be talking and suddenly my step shifts to match the beat, and then my whole rhythm of walking gets re-organized. I also adjust other people's posture without thinking — I'll see someone with their shoulders rolled forward and I literally feel the asymmetry in my own back. I have to fight the urge to physically straighten strangers. My body is constantly reading other people's bodies and trying to correct them.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain how a person finds their balance after they trip but don't fall. Most people think it's reflex. What actually happens is the body cascades through tiny corrections — the foot lands slightly off, the hip rotates to absorb it, the opposite arm extends as a counterweight, the head finds the vertical because the inner ear is screaming about it. Each correction triggers the next. It's a chain of micro-decisions made by parts of you that don't ask permission. By the time your mind notices you almost fell, your body has already filed the whole event and moved on.
Q4 — Conflict: My yoga teacher and I got into a hot argument once because she kept correcting my downward dog by pushing my hips back. I told her, calmly the first time and not calmly the third, that her correction was making my hamstring scream and that my version was actually balanced for my body. She told me I was being defensive. The surface story was about a yoga pose. The real story was that I trusted my body's information more than her external assessment, and she experienced that as insubordination. I never went back.
Q5 — Sound: The sound of bare feet on a stage from underneath — I went to a dance performance once where the stage was elevated and I sat in the front row. The pads of the dancers' feet made this very specific low thud, and the rhythm of their leaps was a kind of muffled percussion through the floor. It hit me in my chest. I cried for a reason I couldn't name. It was like hearing my own heartbeat externalized and synchronized with other heartbeats. I think about that sound a lot.
Q6 — Body: I was playing pickup basketball in college and the ball came toward me too high. Before I'd processed where the ball was, my legs had loaded and my back had arched and my hands were extended at exactly the right angle. I caught the ball and threw it back in one motion that I didn't author. People often say things like 'good catch' when they see this, but it wasn't a catch — I had nothing to do with it. My body had decided three full seconds before my mind had any input.
Q7 — Unworded: The exact feeling of your body being where it's supposed to be in space. Not where your mind tells it to be — where it actually IS. There's a quiet that happens when those two things align. Walking down the street, you can feel out of joint with your own body and not know it, then suddenly something shifts and you're inhabiting yourself completely. It's not about energy or fitness. It's about presence. I've never found words for it but it might be the single most pleasant sensation I know.
